content.text: Operators combine values into expressions that drive agent decisions: comparing tool latencies, combining boolean guard flags, and building derived configuration. Understanding precedence, short-circuit behaviour, and identity versus equality prevents logic bugs in multi-step agent loops.
is a symbol or keyword that performs an operation on one or more
(the values or expressions it acts upon). An
is any syntactically valid combination of values, variables, operators, and function calls that the Python interpreter can reduce to a single value.
Python's operator model is uniform: every operator ultimately calls a
). This means custom classes can override any operator behaviour, which matters when building domain objects for AI pipelines.
— return actual operand values, not booleans
— O(1) on sets/dicts, O(n) on lists
— tests object identity, not value equality

Agent control flow depends entirely on expressions. Every retry decision, every tool-selection guard, every confidence threshold comparison is ultimately a Python expression evaluated at runtime. A misplaced

, or a wrong precedence assumption can silently route every request down the wrong branch — producing hard-to-reproduce bugs in production pipelines where state is complex and execution is non-linear.
Operators also appear inside data structures. Dict unions (

), set intersections for tool allowlists, and bitwise flags for permission masks all rely on the same operator machinery. Understanding the underlying dunder-method system means you can read library code and write clean, idiomatic Python across every layer of the agentic stack.
— compound expressions encode routing policy in a single statement that reads almost like English
skip expensive sub-expressions, avoiding unnecessary API calls or DB queries
is valid Python and reads exactly like a mathematical interval
— assign and test in one expression, eliminating boilerplate in streaming loops and conditional pipelines
for concise configuration merging without repeated
classes) can support natural comparison syntax without extra helper methods
— dense expressions without parentheses silently evaluate in unexpected order, e.g.
in Python; confidence scores and cost thresholds must use
— empty string, zero, and empty list are all falsy; implicit truthiness tests hide type mismatches in agent policy code
— a variable assigned inside a walrus expression remains in scope after the loop, which can cause subtle reuse bugs
mutates the list in place via
creates a new object — different semantics despite identical syntax
Python evaluates operators in a fixed precedence order. Knowing the levels prevents accidental misreadings in guard expressions:
==, !=, <, >, <=, >=, in, not in, is, is not
Comparison + membership + identity — all same level, chainable
— they return the actual operand that determined the result. This enables idiomatic default-value patterns widely used in agent configuration:
— classic default pattern; works because empty string is falsy
Two critical operators are often confused.
and tests whether the values are equivalent.
tests whether both names point to the exact same object in memory (same
). CPython interns small integers and short strings, so
for string literals — a coincidence, not a guarantee.

# Identity vs equality — a critical distinction
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
c = a

print(a == b)   # True  — same values
print(a is b)   # False — different list objects in memory
print(a is c)   # True  — c is just another name for the same list

# The only correct use of `is`:
result = None
if result is None:        # CORRECT: singleton check
    print("no result yet")

# Dangerous: string interning makes this work in CPython
# but it is NOT guaranteed across all implementations
model = "gpt-4o"
if model is "gpt-4o":     # WRONG: use == for strings
    pass

# Augmented assignment and mutation
original = [1, 2]
copy = original
copy += [3]          # mutates via __iadd__; original is also [1, 2, 3]
print(original)      # [1, 2, 3]  — surprise!

tuple_a = (1, 2)
tuple_b = tuple_a
tuple_b += (3,)      # creates NEW tuple; tuple_a is unchanged
print(tuple_a)       # (1, 2)  — tuples are immutable

Below is a set of patterns covering the most common operator scenarios in agentic Python code, including guard expressions, streaming loops, and configuration merging.

# ---- Arithmetic operators in cost accounting ----
INPUT_COST_PER_1K = 0.0015   # USD per 1K input tokens
OUTPUT_COST_PER_1K = 0.006

def estimate_cost(input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    input_cost  = (input_tokens  / 1_000) * INPUT_COST_PER_1K
    output_cost = (output_tokens / 1_000) * OUTPUT_COST_PER_1K
    total = input_cost + output_cost
    # Floor division: how many full cent-blocks does this cost?
    cent_blocks = total // 0.01
    return round(total, 6)

# ---- Comparison chaining in routing ----

def classify_request(token_count: int, complexity_score: float) -> str:
    if token_count > 32_000 or complexity_score > 0.9:
        return "large"
    if 8_000 < token_count <= 32_000 or 0.6 < complexity_score <= 0.9:
        return "medium"
    return "small"

# ---- Logical operators returning non-booleans ----

env_model = None            # not set in environment
fallback  = "gpt-4o-mini"

active_model = env_model or fallback   # 'gpt-4o-mini' because env_model is None
print(active_model)                    # gpt-4o-mini

# Careful: 0 is falsy, so this pattern fails for numeric defaults
retry_count = 0
effective_retries = retry_count or 3   # BUG: should be 0, gives 3!
# Fix: use explicit None sentinel instead
retry_count_explicit: int | None = None
effective_retries = retry_count_explicit if retry_count_explicit is not None else 3

# ---- Bitwise operators for dict/set operations (Python 3.9+) ----

base_tools  = {"web_search", "calculator"}
extra_tools = {"code_exec", "file_read"}
all_tools   = base_tools | extra_tools    # union
shared      = base_tools & extra_tools    # intersection (empty here)

base_cfg  = {"temperature": 0.7, "model": "gpt-4o-mini"}
patch_cfg = {"temperature": 0.0, "max_tokens": 512}
final_cfg = base_cfg | patch_cfg   # patch wins on overlapping keys
print(final_cfg)  # {'temperature': 0.0, 'model': 'gpt-4o-mini', 'max_tokens': 512}

A production-grade model router applies a cascade of operator-driven conditions to select the cheapest model that still satisfies quality constraints, merges per-request configuration, and enforces a cost ceiling before dispatching:

"""model_router.py — operator-driven routing logic used in a multi-agent pipeline."""
from __future__ import annotations
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

COMPLEX_TASKS = {"code_review", "multi_file_edit", "security_audit", "architecture_plan"}
FATAL_ERROR_CODES = {"auth_failed", "quota_exceeded", "invalid_key"}

@dataclass
class RoutingContext:
    task_type: str
    estimated_tokens: int
    budget_usd: float
    attempt: int = 0
    last_error: str | None = None
    overrides: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

BASE_MODEL_CONFIG = {
    "large":  {"model": "gpt-4o",       "cost_per_1k": 0.030},
    "medium": {"model": "gpt-4o-mini",  "cost_per_1k": 0.006},
    "small":  {"model": "gpt-3.5-turbo","cost_per_1k": 0.001},
}

def select_model(ctx: RoutingContext) -> str | None:
    """Return model name or None if routing should abort."""
    # Abort on fatal error (membership operator)
    if ctx.last_error is not None and ctx.last_error in FATAL_ERROR_CODES:
        return None

    # Abort when retry budget is exhausted (comparison operator)
    if ctx.attempt >= 5:
        return None

    # Select tier using chained comparisons + membership
    is_complex   = ctx.task_type in COMPLEX_TASKS
    needs_large  = is_complex and ctx.estimated_tokens > 8_000
    needs_medium = (not needs_large) and ctx.estimated_tokens > 2_000
    tier = "large" if needs_large else ("medium" if needs_medium else "small")

    # Cost gate: estimated cost must not exceed remaining budget
    cfg = BASE_MODEL_CONFIG[tier]
    est_cost = (ctx.estimated_tokens / 1_000) * cfg["cost_per_1k"]
    if est_cost > ctx.budget_usd:
        # Downgrade one tier to stay within budget
        if tier == "large":
            tier = "medium"
        elif tier == "medium":
            tier = "small"
        else:
            return None   # even small model is too expensive

    # Merge runtime overrides (dict union, right side wins)
    final_cfg = BASE_MODEL_CONFIG[tier] | ctx.overrides
    return final_cfg["model"]


# --- Example usage ---
ctx = RoutingContext(
    task_type="code_review",
    estimated_tokens=12_000,
    budget_usd=0.50,
    overrides={"temperature": 0.0},
)
model = select_model(ctx)
print(model)  # gpt-4o — large tier, within $0.50 budget

ctx_tight = RoutingContext(
    task_type="code_review",
    estimated_tokens=12_000,
    budget_usd=0.05,   # tight budget forces downgrade
)
print(select_model(ctx_tight))  # gpt-4o-mini — medium tier after downgrade

often appears to work but will fail with dynamically created strings or large integers
replaces a legitimate zero with 5; fix by checking
mutates lists in-place, so two variables pointing to the same list both reflect the change unexpectedly
inside a comprehension is visible in the enclosing function scope, which can cause accidental reuse
Extract complex guard conditions into named booleans
makes compound conditions self-documenting and individually testable
— eliminates any ambiguity and lets the reader skip the precedence table
for all floating-point threshold tests — confidence scores, cost estimates, and similarity measures all require tolerance
over falsy defaults when 0, empty string, or empty list are valid values
against sets, not lists, for membership checks on allowlists that might grow
loops and list comprehensions where the pattern is established — avoid it in complex nested expressions
Put the most expensive sub-expression last in an
chain — short-circuiting ensures it is only evaluated when all cheaper conditions pass
In tight loops and high-throughput pipelines, operator choice has measurable impact:
is O(n) — 100x slower for a 100-item list
, the expensive call is skipped when the cheap check fails; reversing the order makes every iteration pay the expensive cost
) is O(k) for the appended elements (amortised O(1) per element) — much faster than repeated
which creates a new list each time

provides a logical merge without copying
Walrus in comprehensions avoids double evaluation
— without walrus, you would need to call the same function twice (once to filter, once to use the value), or pre-compute to a temporary list
— catch operator type errors at development time: applying
to a string and an int, or comparing incompatible types with
used for literals, and comparison to boolean singletons
) — exposes all operators as first-class functions (
returns a boolean array, not a scalar; do not use Python
import dis; dis.dis(lambda: a and b or c)
— useful for understanding exactly when short-circuits fire
; f-strings are clearer and avoid O(n²) string copies in loops
— for allowlists checked on every request, convert to a set once at startup; for small, ordered data, a list
is laziest (no copy) but changes propagate through
— walrus is concise but only valid in Python 3.8+; in older code or when readability suffers, pre-compute to a named variable before the conditional
because it avoids the Python function-call overhead for the lambda body

— it executes arbitrary Python, creating remote code execution risk. Use
for safe evaluation of literals from config, or a proper parser for expression DSLs
Integer overflow: Python integers are arbitrary precision
— no overflow, but very large exponents (
) can cause out-of-memory; guard token-count arithmetic with reasonable ceilings
passes for any non-empty string, including '
silently ignores an empty-but-valid result from

Think of an expression as a small pipeline: raw operands flow in from left to right, each operator is a transformation stage applied in precedence order, and one final value flows out. Logical operators (
— they can stop the pipeline early and return an intermediate value. Parentheses are brackets that force a sub-pipeline to complete before feeding into the outer one. Identity (
) asks "same box in memory?", while equality (
When in doubt about precedence, add parentheses. Python's parser is the ground truth, but your colleagues' mental model is what matters in code review.
Know the seven operator categories and when each is appropriate; use identity (
Parentheses always win on precedence; add them whenever mixing
Short-circuit evaluation skips unnecessary work — order conditions from cheapest to most expensive
for all threshold checks on confidence scores, costs, and similarities
for O(1) allowlist membership checks; list
is O(n) and degrades as the list grows
Extract complex guard conditions into named booleans — improves readability, testability, and error messages
) assigns and tests in one step — ideal for streaming loops and filtered comprehensions, but keep its use visible and local
Agent policy code benefits most from explicit, readable expressions — the correctness of routing logic outweighs any micro-optimisation from clever operator chaining
